params.text:
Privacy and dignity: the heart of good care

Every person you support has a life, a history and a sense of who they are that reaches far beyond the care they need. Standard 7 of the Care Certificate asks you to protect two things that make people feel human even when they depend on others: their privacy and their dignity.

Privacy means having space that is your own — freedom from unwanted attention, and control over your body, your belongings and your personal information. Dignity means being treated as a person of worth: spoken to with respect, listened to, and never made to feel like a burden, an object or a task on a list.

These are not extras you add when you have time. They are the difference between care that keeps someone alive and care that helps someone live well.

Why it matters so much

When someone needs help with washing, dressing, eating or using the toilet, they are often in the most exposed moments of their day. How you behave in those moments shapes how they feel about themselves.

  • Care that respects dignity builds confidence, trust and self-esteem.
  • Care that ignores it can leave people feeling ashamed, powerless and afraid to ask for help.
  • Small things — a knock, a closed door, using someone's chosen name — carry a large message: you matter here.

Privacy and dignity are also legal duties, not just kindness. The rest of this course looks at what they mean in practice and how the law and good-practice frameworks back you up.
